dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce Provided with programmes for supporting staff with regards to mental health, injuries, medical treatment and related support measures?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce Provided
dataElements.description: 1: No Support:There are no programs or formal support structures in place for addressing staff mental health, injuries, medical treatment, or related needs. Staff must seek assistance on their own, with no organizational support. 2: Minimal Support:Some informal support is available, such as occasional guidance or limited resources, but there are no structured programs for mental health, injuries, or medical treatment. Assistance is sporadic and inconsistent. 3: Basic Support:Basic programs exist for addressing staff mental health and physical injuries, but they are limited in scope. The organization provides minimal medical treatment or related support, and these programs may not be comprehensive or widely promoted. 4: Moderate Support:A structured set of programs is in place, offering support for staff mental health, injuries, and medical treatment. These programs are functional, addressing key areas of staff well-being, but may lack advanced features like regular assessments or personalized support. 5: Full Support:Comprehensive, well-coordinated programs are provided to support staff mental health, injuries, medical treatment, and other related needs. These programs are proactive, widely accessible, and regularly updated, ensuring staff receive full emotional, medical, and rehabilitative support, with mechanisms for early intervention and personalized care.

dataElements.name:
flood-hw-hr-#1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with an emergency plan for shift relay or replacement of health professionals to 
 ensure that staff get adequate rest?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-#1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce Equippe
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in place:No emergency plan exists for shift relay or replacement of health professionals. Staff work without structured breaks or replacements, increasing risk of fatigue and burnout. 2: Minimal Plan in place:There is some recognition of the need for an emergency plan, but no formal plan has been created. Shift relays or replacements are handled reactively, without clear guidelines. 3: Basic plan in place:A basic emergency plan is in place, outlining general procedures for shift relay or replacement. However, it lacks clear protocols for ensuring adequate staff rest, and the plan may not be consistently applied. Only a few PHC staff are familiar with the plan. 4: Moderately developed plan in place:A structured emergency plan is developed and includes defined procedures for shift relay or replacement of health professionals. It ensures staff get adequate rest, but there may be room for improvement in terms of flexibility or response during unexpected crises. All PHC staff are familiar with the plan. 5: Fully Developed plan in place:A comprehensive and regularly updated emergency plan is fully operational, ensuring that health professionals have a seamless shift relay or replacement system. The plan prioritizes staff well-being, guaranteeing adequate rest periods, with contingencies in place to manage sudden or large-scale staff shortages effectively. All PHC staff are familiar with the plan. PHC able to mention Use Case/s

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce Prepared with a contingency plan for accessing additional health workforce to strengthen performance capacity?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce Prepared
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation:There is no contingency plan for accessing additional health workforce. The organization is unprepared to address workforce shortages, with no strategy in place to respond to increased demand. 2: Minimal Preparation:Some awareness of the need for a contingency plan exists, but no formal plan has been developed. There may be informal discussions or ideas, but nothing concrete is in place. 3: Basic Preparation:A contingency plan is developed with basic strategies for accessing additional health workforce. However, the plan is not comprehensive and may lack detailed processes, resources, or scalability. 4: Moderate Preparation:A well-defined contingency plan is in place with clear procedures and resources identified for accessing additional health workforce. The plan is functional but may still have room for improvement in terms of speed and flexibility. 5: Full Preparation:A comprehensive, regularly updated contingency plan is fully integrated into the organization’s operations. It includes clear, actionable steps for quickly accessing additional health workforce, with established partnerships and resources ready for deployment to ensure maximum performance capacity during peak demand or emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce Provided with an information system to manage occupational safety and health in the facility during a flood?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce Provided
dataElements.description: 1: No System:There is no information system in place to manage occupational safety and health during a flood. Staff and management rely on ad-hoc measures with no structured data or guidance available. 2: Minimal System:An initial awareness exists of the need for an information system, but it is either not developed or only partially available. Limited and uncoordinated information is shared during a flood situation, making safety management inefficient. 3: Basic System:A basic information system exists for managing occupational safety and health during a flood. It covers essential aspects but lacks advanced functionality such as real-time updates, automated alerts, or comprehensive reporting. Information may not be fully accessible to all relevant staff. 4: Moderate System:A moderately developed information system is in place, offering solid functionality for managing occupational safety and health during floods. It provides real-time updates, clear guidance, and supports decision-making, but may still have room for improvement in areas like user accessibility, integration with other safety measures, or predictive features. 5: Fully Integrated System:A comprehensive, fully integrated information system is available to manage occupational safety and health during a flood. It includes real-time alerts, automated decision support, robust data reporting, accessible communication to all staff levels, and seamless integration with emergency response procedures. The system is regularly updated, tested, and ensures maximum protection of employees during flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with an emergency plan to protect health workers from multiple biological and chemical hazards?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce Equipped
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in place:No emergency plan exists to protect health workers from biological and chemical hazards. Workers are left vulnerable in the event of exposure to such hazards, with no structured response in place. 2: Minimal Plan:There is some recognition of the need for an emergency plan, but it is either undeveloped or informal. Limited protective measures are in place, and the response to biological and chemical hazards is uncoordinated and largely reactive. 3: Basic Plan in place:A basic emergency plan is in place, outlining general procedures to protect health workers from biological and chemical hazards. The plan covers some key risks but lacks comprehensive details, and implementation may be inconsistent. 4: Moderate Plan:A structured emergency plan exists, addressing multiple biological and chemical hazards with defined procedures for protecting health workers. The plan includes protective equipment, training, and protocols, but may still require improvements in its adaptability to complex or large-scale hazard scenarios. 5: Full Preparation:A comprehensive, regularly updated emergency plan is fully operational, ensuring health workers are thoroughly protected from multiple biological and chemical hazards. The plan includes detailed risk assessments, robust safety protocols, advanced protective measures, and regular drills, ensuring readiness and optimal worker safety under a wide range of hazardous conditions.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.6 Is the Health Workforce Provided with a post-flood employee recovery assistance programme according to staff needs?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.6 Is the Health Workforce Provided
dataElements.description: 1: No Assistance program in place:"No post-flood recovery information provided, no efforts in place to address individual staff needs after flood or any emergency situation." 2: Minimal-Level Assistance:"General post-flood employee recovery programme offered, but only partially addresses individual staff needs." 3: Basic-level Assistance:"Post-flood employee recovery assistance programme available, with some customization to meet general staff needs." 4: Moderately High-Level Assistance:"Comprehensive post-flood recovery programme implemented, tailored to specific staff needs based on assessments." 5: Full/Maximum Assistance:"Fully personalized post-flood employee recovery assistance programme designed to meet every staff member's unique needs, with ongoing support."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.7 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with a coordinated plan, including volunteers on standby, to assist during an emergency or to support health professionals?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.7 Is the Health Workforce Equipped
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:No coordinated plan exists, and no volunteers are available to assist during an emergency or support health professionals 2: Minimal Plan:A basic plan is outlined, but coordination is weak and volunteers are not organized or on standby for emergencies. 3: Basic Plan:A partially coordinated plan is in place, with some volunteers identified and on standby, though their readiness and involvement in supporting health professionals are limited." 4: Moderately Coordinated Plan:A well-structured, coordinated plan is in place, with volunteers trained and actively on standby, ready to assist during an emergency and support health professionals." 5: Fully Coordinated/Comprehensive Plan:A fully integrated and comprehensive emergency plan is operational, with highly trained and regularly engaged volunteers on standby, ready to provide seamless support to health professionals in all emergency situations."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.8 Is the Health Workforce Provided with full personal protective equipment, especially for clean-up crews (including waterproof safety boots, goggles, work gloves and masks)?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.8 Is the Health Workforce Provided
dataElements.description: 1: No Equipment:No personal protective equipment (PPE) is provided to clean-up crews. 2: Minimal Equipment:Only basic protective gear is provided, such as gloves or masks, but key items like waterproof boots and goggles are missing. 3: Basic Equipment:Some essential PPE is provided, including masks, gloves, and goggles, but not all necessary items such as waterproof safety boots. 4: Moderate Adequate Equipment:Clean-up crews are provided with most of the necessary PPE, including masks, gloves, goggles, and waterproof safety boots, though there may be occasional shortages. 5: Full Equipment:Clean-up crews are fully equipped with all required PPE, including waterproof safety boots, goggles, work gloves, and masks, ensuring comprehensive protection.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-hr-1.1.9 Is the Health Workforce Provided with safe water and food during an event?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-hr-1.1.9 Is the Health Workforce Provided
dataElements.description: 1: No Provision:No safe water or food is provided during the event. 2: Minimal Provision:"Limited and inconsistent access to safe water or food is provided, with no guarantee of availability throughout the event." 3: Basic Provision:Safe water and food are available, but only in limited quantities and may not be sufficient for the entire duration of the event. 4: Moderate Adequate Provision:Safe water and food are consistently provided in sufficient quantities, but with minimal variety or options. 5: Full/Comprehensive Provision:"Ample and varied safe water and food are provided throughout the event, ensuring all participants have full and continuous access to necessary supplies."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce Trained on public health and climate change hazards including health impacts related to floods
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce Trained
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:No training is provided on public health or climate change hazards, including health impacts related to floods. 2: Minimal Training:Basic awareness is raised on public health or climate change hazards, but no formal training is conducted on the health impacts related to floods. 3: Basic/Introductory Training:Some training is provided on public health and climate change hazards, with a limited focus on the health impacts of floods, but coverage is not comprehensive. 4: Moderate Training:Employees receive structured training on public health and climate change hazards, including a solid focus on health impacts related to floods, though the depth of training is moderate. 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:Extensive and detailed training is provided on public health and climate change hazards, with in-depth coverage of health impacts related to floods, ensuring thorough understanding and preparedness."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.2 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with knowledge, experience, training and resources to manage flood risk reduction at the facility and in the local communities?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.2 Is the Health Workforce Equipped
dataElements.description: 1: No Knowledge or Resources:Has no knowledge, experience, training, or resources to manage flood risk reduction at the facility or in the local communities 2: Minimal Knowledge and Resources:Possesses basic awareness of flood risk reduction, but lacks formal training, experience, and access to resources necessary for effective management." 3: Basic Knowledge and Resources:Has some knowledge and limited training on flood risk reduction, with minimal experience and scarce resources available for managing risks at the facility or in the local communities 4: Moderate Knowledge and Resources:Equipped with a good level of knowledge, experience, and training to manage flood risk reduction, along with access to moderate resources, though some gaps in capacity may remain. 5: Full Knowledge and Resources:Fully equipped with extensive knowledge, significant experience, thorough training, and ample resources to effectively manage flood risk reduction at the facility and in local communities.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce Engaged in the development of plans and responses to flood risk
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce Engaged
dataElements.description: 1: No Engagement:Not engaged in the development of any plans or responses to flood risk. 2: Minimal Engagement:Has minimal involvement in flood risk planning, providing occasional input but not actively participating in the process. 3: Basic Engagement:Involved in some aspects of flood risk planning and response development, but participation is irregular and limited to specific tasks. 4: Moderate Active Engagement:Actively participates in the development of flood risk plans and responses, contributing to key decisions and processes, though not leading the efforts. 5: Full Engagement:Fully engaged in the development of flood risk plans and responses, taking a leadership role in shaping strategies and coordinating efforts.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce Prepared and able to implement risk reduction actions for protecting themselves?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce Prepared
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation:Not prepared or able to implement any risk reduction actions for self-protection. 2: Minimal Preparation:Has basic awareness of risk reduction actions but lacks the ability or resources to effectively implement them for self-protection. 3: Basic Preparation:Somewhat prepared, with knowledge of risk reduction actions, but has limited ability or resources to fully implement them. 4: Moderate Preparation:Prepared and able to implement most risk reduction actions for self-protection, though there may be gaps in readiness or resources. 5: Full Preparation:Fully prepared and able to effectively implement all necessary risk reduction actions to protect themselves, with the required knowledge, skills, and resources.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce Prepared with a contingency plan for additional health workforce to strengthen performance capacity
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce Prepared
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:No contingency plan exists for mobilizing an additional health workforce to strengthen performance capacity. 2: Minimal Plan:A basic contingency plan is in place, but it lacks clear details and coordination for mobilizing an additional health workforce during emergencies. 3: Basic Plan:A contingency plan exists with some provisions for increasing the health workforce, but it is not comprehensive, and gaps remain in resources, training, or coordination. 4: Moderate Plan:A well-structured contingency plan is in place, with clear strategies to mobilize an additional health workforce. However, there may be limitations in scaling the workforce quickly or efficiently. 5: Full/Comprehensive Plan:A fully developed and detailed contingency plan is in place, ensuring rapid mobilization of a trained additional health workforce to effectively strengthen performance capacity during emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce Prepared with a contingency plan for continuing to provide services at other facilities or in the local communities (health primary care), if necessary?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce Prepared
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:No contingency plan exists for continuing to provide services at other facilities or in local communities. 2: Minimal Plan:A basic contingency plan is in place, but it lacks clear procedures and coordination for continuing services at alternate facilities or in local communities. 3: Basic Plan:A contingency plan exists with provisions for relocating services to other facilities or communities, but it is incomplete, with gaps in resources, communication, or logistical support. 4: Moderate Plan:A structured contingency plan is in place with clear strategies for continuing services at other facilities or in local communities. However, some aspects, like staffing or resources, may be limited in scale or execution. 5: Full/Comprehensive Plan:A fully developed and detailed contingency plan ensures seamless continuation of services at other facilities or within local communities, with all necessary staffing, resources, and logistical support fully prepared.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.7 Is the Health Workforce Trained to detect posttraumatic stress disorder among staff to take prompt action?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.7 Is the Health Workforce Trained
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:Not trained at all to detect PTSD among staff or take any action. 2: Minimal Training:Has minimal awareness of PTSD, but lacks formal training and skills to effectively detect or address it among staff. 3: Basic Training:Has received some basic training on recognizing signs of PTSD among staff, but the ability to take prompt and appropriate action is limited. 4: Moderate Training:Trained to detect common signs of PTSD among staff, with the ability to take prompt action, though not extensively equipped to handle complex cases." 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:Fully trained to detect PTSD among staff, with advanced knowledge and the ability to take immediate and appropriate action, including referrals for professional support."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.8 Is the Health Workforce Trained to manage hazardous chemicals in emergency situations?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.8 Is the Health Workforce Trained
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:Not trained to manage hazardous chemicals in emergency situations. 2: Minimal Training:Has basic awareness of hazardous chemicals, but lacks formal training or the skills to manage them effectively in emergency situations. 3: Basic Training:Has received some training on handling hazardous chemicals in emergencies, but with limited depth and practical experience in managing complex or high-risk scenarios. 4: Moderate Training:Trained to manage hazardous chemicals in emergency situations, with the ability to follow established protocols and respond to most incidents, though complex situations may still require additional support. 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:Fully trained and experienced in managing hazardous chemicals in emergency situations, with the ability to lead responses, handle complex incidents, and ensure safety through advanced protocols and techniques.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.2.9 Is the Health Workforce Trained to an appropriate standard to maintain the correct level of safety of electrical power supply, in both routine and emergency/disaster situations?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.2.9 Is the Health Workforce Trained
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:Not trained to maintain the safety of the electrical power supply in any situation. 2: Minimal Training:Has basic awareness of electrical power safety, but lacks the formal training necessary to maintain safety in routine or emergency situations. 3: Basic Training:Trained to maintain safety standards for electrical power supply in routine situations, but lacks sufficient knowledge and experience to manage emergencies or disaster scenarios effectively. 4: Moderate Training:Trained to an appropriate standard to maintain electrical power safety in both routine and some emergency situations, but may require additional support in complex disaster scenarios. 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:Fully trained to a high standard, capable of maintaining the correct level of electrical power safety in all routine operations and emergency/disaster situations, with advanced knowledge of protocols and contingency measures."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce Provided with a safe internal communication system, specially in emergency situations?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce Provide
dataElements.description: 1: No Communication System:No internal communication system is provided, making it unsafe and unreliable, especially in emergency situations. 2: Minimal Communication System:A basic internal communication system is provided, but it is unreliable and lacks specific safety features for use during emergency situations. 3: Basic Communication System in place:An internal communication system is available with some safety features, but it may not be fully reliable or secure in all emergency situations. 4: Moderately Reliable Communication System:A safe and reliable internal communication system is provided, with features that are suitable for most emergency situations, though there may still be occasional vulnerabilities. 5: Full/ Comprehensive and Secure Communication System:A fully secure, robust internal communication system is in place, designed specifically for safe and effective communication during all types of emergency situations, ensuring full reliability and security.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.3.2 Is the Health Workforce Informed on how to use and follow a surveillance system to track health outcomes?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.3.2 Is the Health Workforce Informed
dataElements.description: 1: No Information:Not informed or aware of how to use or follow a surveillance system to track health outcomes. 2: Minimal Information:Has basic awareness of the existence of a surveillance system but lacks understanding or training on how to use it to track health outcomes. 3: Basic Information:Some information is provided on how to use and follow the surveillance system, but the understanding of its functionality and how to track health outcomes is incomplete. 4: Moderately Adequate Information:Informed on how to use and follow the surveillance system to track health outcomes, with a good level of understanding and ability to use the system effectively in most situations. 5: Full/Comprehensive Information:Fully informed and trained on how to use and follow the surveillance system to track health outcomes, with advanced knowledge of the system’s features and the ability to leverage it effectively in all relevant scenarios.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce Aware of contingency plans for accessing and leaving the facility during flood emergencies, and health workforce transportation?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce Aware o
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:Not aware of any contingency plans for accessing or leaving the facility during flood emergencies, including health workforce transportation. 2: Minimal Awareness:Has heard of the existence of contingency plans for flood emergencies but is not familiar with the details or how they apply to accessing or leaving the facility. 3: BAsic Awareness:Some awareness of contingency plans for accessing and leaving the facility during flood emergencies, but lacks comprehensive knowledge of transportation arrangements for the health workforce. 4: Moderate Awareness:Well-informed about the contingency plans for accessing and leaving the facility during flood emergencies, with a clear understanding of the transportation provisions for the health workforce. 5: Full Awareness:Fully aware of the comprehensive contingency plans, including detailed knowledge of how to access and leave the facility during flood emergencies, with complete understanding of health workforce transportation logistics.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd-1.3.4 Is the Health Workforce Regularly participating in community disaster planning committees to: improve knowledge on how to reduce risks, be prepared and respond to floods, and recover better than before through
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd-1.3.4 Is the Health Workforce Regularl
dataElements.description: 1: No Participation:Does not participate in any community disaster planning committees. 2: Minimal Participation:Occasionally attends community disaster planning meetings but is not actively involved in discussions or decision-making. 3: Basic Participation:Participates in some community disaster planning committees with irregular involvement, contributing to discussions on risk reduction, preparedness, and response, but without consistent engagement. 4: Moderate Active Participation:Regularly participates in community disaster planning committees, contributing knowledge on flood risk reduction, preparedness, response, and recovery, and actively involved in discussions and planning. 5: Full and Consistent Participation:Fully engaged in community disaster planning committees, consistently contributing to discussions and initiatives to reduce risks, improve flood preparedness, response, and implement adaptation measures for better recovery.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce Prepared with clear messaging about water and food safety during and after a flood?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce Prepare
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation:No messaging is provided regarding water and food safety during or after a flood. 2: Minimal Preparation:Some basic messaging exists, but it is unclear, inconsistent, or insufficiently communicated to staff or the public regarding water and food safety during and after a flood. 3: Basic Preparation:Messaging on water and food safety is provided, but it may not be fully comprehensive or timely. Information is somewhat clear but could be improved in detail and distribution during and after a flood. 4: Moderately Adequate Preparation:Clear and reliable messaging about water and food safety is in place, communicated to the necessary parties during and after a flood. Most people understand the information, though there may be room for improvement in ensuring full reach and timeliness 5: Full/ Comprehensive Preparation:Fully prepared with clear, comprehensive, and timely messaging about water and food safety during and after a flood. Messaging is well-distributed, consistently updated, and fully understood by staff and the public, ensuring safety and awareness.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd- 1.3.6 Is the Health Workforce Prepared with clear messaging, and staff trained on exit and evacuation routes that are clearly marked and free of obstacles to enable emergency evacuation)?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd- 1.3.6 Is the Health Workforce Prepare
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation:No clear messaging or training provided on exit and evacuation routes. Routes are unmarked and may have obstacles, posing risks during emergency evacuation. 2: Minimal Preparation:Basic awareness of evacuation routes exists, but clear messaging and formal staff training are lacking. Evacuation routes may be partially marked but still contain obstacles. 3: Basic Preparation:Some messaging is in place, and staff have received limited training on exit and evacuation routes. Most routes are marked, but some may still have obstacles, and readiness is not fully assured. 4: Moderately Adequate Preparation:Clear messaging is provided, and staff are adequately trained on marked exit and evacuation routes. Routes are generally free of obstacles, though occasional checks may be needed to ensure full preparedness." 5: Full/ Comprehensive Preparation:Fully prepared with clear and consistent messaging, and all staff are thoroughly trained on clearly marked, well-maintained, and obstacle-free evacuation routes. Regular drills ensure readiness for emergency evacuation."

dataElements.name: flood-hw-car- 1.3.7 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with a flood plan or programme with clear instructions on how to proceed during flood emergency situations?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-car- 1.3.7 Is the Health Workforce Equipp
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:No flood plan or program exists, and there are no instructions on how to proceed during flood emergency situations. 2: Minimal Plan:A very basic flood plan exists, but the instructions are unclear or incomplete, offering limited guidance on how to proceed during flood emergencies. 3: Basic Plan:A flood plan or program is in place with some clear instructions, but it may lack comprehensive details, leaving some areas of response underprepared during a flood emergency. 4: Moderately Adequate Plan:An established flood plan or program is in place, providing mostly clear instructions on how to proceed during a flood emergency, with adequate guidance for most situations 5: Full/ Comprehensive Plan:Fully equipped with a detailed flood plan or program, including clear, comprehensive instructions on how to proceed during any flood emergency situation, ensuring complete preparedness and effective response

dataElements.name: flood-hw-cd- 1.3.8 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with a community health educational programme to assist the community to reduce vulnerabilities to flood impacts?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-cd- 1.3.8 Is the Health Workforce Equippe
dataElements.description: 1: No Educational Program:No community health educational program exists to assist in reducing vulnerabilities to flood impacts 2: Minimal Program:A very basic educational program is in place, but it lacks clear objectives or sufficient resources to effectively help the community reduce vulnerabilities to flood impacts 3: Basic Program:A community health educational program exists, offering some useful information, but it is not fully comprehensive, and there are gaps in the content or reach, limiting its impact on reducing vulnerabilities to flood risks 4: Moderately Adequate Program:A well-developed educational program is in place, providing clear and helpful information to the community on reducing vulnerabilities to flood impacts, with moderate coverage and effectiveness 5: Full/Comprehensive Program:Fully equipped with a comprehensive community health educational program, designed to thoroughly assist the community in reducing vulnerabilities to flood impacts, with widespread participation and significant impact on community preparedness and resilience.

dataElements.name: flood-hw-car- 1.3.9 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with a community health educational programme to improve community health in the face of flood risks?
dataElements.shortName: flood-hw-car- 1.3.9 Is the Health Workforce Equipp
dataElements.description: 1: No Educational Program:No community health educational program exists to improve community health in response to flood risks 2: Minimal Program:A very basic educational program exists, but it provides limited information and resources, making minimal impact on improving community health in the face of flood risks. 3: Basic Program:A community health educational program is in place, but it offers only partial coverage or content. It provides some useful information, but lacks the depth and reach needed to effectively improve community health in flood risk situations. 4: Moderately Adequate Program:A well-structured educational program exists, providing clear and actionable guidance to the community on improving health in response to flood risks. It has reasonable coverage and moderately improves community resilience to health challenges during floods 5: Full/Comprehensive Program:Fully equipped with a comprehensive community health educational program, designed to significantly improve community health in the face of flood risks. The program is well-resourced, widely accessible, and highly effective in preparing and protecting community health during flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.1. Does the health care facility have an updated assessment plan to map risks to the sanitation infrastructure in place, and to identify where services could be disrupted from floods?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.1. Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The healthcare facility does not have any assessment plan to map risks to the sanitation infrastructure or identify potential service disruptions due to floods. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:The healthcare facility has begun discussions or considerations for developing an assessment plan but has not yet formalised or implemented one. 3: Basic plan drafted:A draft assessment plan exists to map risks to the sanitation infrastructure, but it has not been fully finalised, implemented, or updated to reflect current flood risks. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:The healthcare facility has an assessment plan to map risks to sanitation infrastructure and identify potential flood disruptions, but it has not been recently updated or reviewed. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:The healthcare facility has a fully updated assessment plan in place that regularly maps risks to the sanitation infrastructure and actively identifies where services could be disrupted by floods. This plan is reviewed and revised periodically.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.2 Does the health care facility verify water safety conditions, including updated risk assessments to map water resources and water supplies for the facility?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.2 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Verification:No system is in place to verify water safety or conduct risk assessments. 2: Minimal Verification:Initial discussions or actions are being considered to verify water safety and assess risks. 3: Basic Verification:Some aspects of water safety are verified, but risk assessments are incomplete or outdated. 4: Moderate Verification, Not Fully Updated:A verification process exists but is not regularly updated or thoroughly conducted. 5: Full/ Comprehensive verification:Water safety conditions are regularly verified, and updated risk assessments for water resources and supplies are conducted.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.3 Does the health care facility have a quality monitoring plan for drinking water during and after the event?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.3 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Monitoring Plan:No plan exists to monitor drinking water quality during or after flood events. 2: Minimal Monitoring plan:A plan is being developed, but it is incomplete or not implemented. 3: Basic Monitoring plan:A monitoring plan is in place but lacks full execution or updates. 4: Moderate monitoring plan:The facility monitors drinking water quality but does not regularly update or improve its plan. 5: Full Monitoring plan:A comprehensive monitoring plan is in place, fully operational, and updated regularly to ensure drinking water safety during and after flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.4 Does the health care facility regularly assess its sanitation system for any possible damage in the event of flooding?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.4 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment:The facility does not regularly assess its sanitation system for potential flood damage. 2: Minimal Assessment:Some assessment procedures exist but are irregular or incomplete. 3: Basic Assessments:Assessments are done periodically but not with consistency or up-to-date methods. 4: Moderate Assessments:Regular assessments are conducted but do not cover all aspects of sanitation infrastructure. 5: Full Assessments:The sanitation system is regularly and comprehensively assessed for damage, particularly during flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.5 Does the health care facility monitor sewer overflows in order to fix pumps in advance of the flood season?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.5 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Monitoring:No system exists to monitor sewer overflows or fix pumps in advance of flood season. 2: Minimal Monitoring:Monitoring is done occasionally, but there’s no systematic approach to fixing pumps before floods. 3: Basic Monitoring:Sewer overflows are monitored in some areas but without consistency or preventive action. 4: Moderate Monitoring:Sewer overflows are regularly monitored, but the plan to fix pumps is outdated 5: Full Monitoring:Regular and proactive monitoring of sewer overflows is done, and pumps are fixed well in advance of the flood season.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.6 Does the health care facility regularly verify safety conditions and proper functioning of all elements of the water distribution system, including storage tanks, cisterns, valves, pipes and connections, and
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.6 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Verification:No checks are done on the water distribution system. 2: Minimal Verification:Some elements of the water system are checked sporadically but not systematically. 3: Basic Verification:Certain elements, such as storage tanks or valves, are verified, but others are overlooked. 4: Moderate Verification:Regular checks are done, but some key elements are not consistently assessed. 5: Full Verification:The facility regularly and thoroughly verifies the safety and functionality of all parts of the water distribution system, including tanks, pipes, and water disinfection processes.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-maa-2.1.7 Does the health care facility have information on water system installation that ensures lower risk of contamination?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-maa-2.1.7 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Information:The facility does not have information on water system installation to ensure lower contamination risks. 2: Minimal Information:Some information is available but incomplete or outdated. 3: Basic Information:Information on water system installation is available, but does not cover all risk mitigation areas. 4: Moderate Information:The facility has detailed information, but it is not reviewed or updated regularly. 5: Full Information:The facility has detailed, up-to-date information on water system installations to minimise contamination risks.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.1.8 Does the health care facility have a natural floodwater infiltration system to reduce risk of facility flooding?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.1.8 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Audits:The healthcare facility does not conduct waste audits. 2: Minimal Audits:Waste audits are conducted sporadically but without a systematic approach to minimising waste. 3: Basic Audits:Waste audits are conducted in some areas, but they do not comprehensively cover all sources of waste. 4: Moderate Audits:Regular audits are conducted but focus on only certain types of waste or processes. 5: Full Audits:Regular and comprehensive waste audits are conducted to reduce waste as much as possible, and corrective actions are taken based on the audit results.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.1. Does the health care facility have a natural floodwater infiltration system to reduce risk of facility flooding?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.1. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No System:No measures are in place for floodwater infiltration. 2: Minimal System:Discussions or plans to create a system have been initiated. 3: Basic System:Some floodwater infiltration systems are in place, but they are not comprehensive. 4: Moderate System:The facility has a system but it requires improvements or updates to function optimally. 5: Full System:A comprehensive natural floodwater infiltration system is installed, fully functional, and regularly updated to mitigate flood risks.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.2. Does the health care facility have anti-mosquito breeding measures?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.2. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Measures:The facility has no anti-mosquito breeding measures in place. 2: Minimal Measures:Some sporadic actions to reduce mosquito breeding are taken, but no formal plan exists. 3: Basic Measures:Measures are in place, but they are not implemented consistently. 4: Moderate Measures:The facility has regular anti-mosquito measures, but they are not comprehensive or frequently reviewed. 5: Full measure in place:The facility has a well-established and regularly updated anti-mosquito breeding programme, covering all risk areas.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.3 Does the health care facility have a schedule for emptying latrines in advance of the flood season to avoid overflows?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.3 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Schedule:There is no schedule or plan in place for emptying latrines before the flood season. 2: Minimal Schedule:Latrines are emptied sporadically without following a regular schedule. 3: Basic Schedule:The facility has a schedule, but it is not always followed or covers only some latrines. 4: Moderate Schedule in Place:The facility has a schedule, but it is not adhered to consistently before each flood season. 5: Full Schedule:The facility consistently follows a schedule for latrine emptying, ensuring no overflows during flood season.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.4. Does the health care facility have a safe health care waste storage place?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.4. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Storage:There is no designated or safe place for health care waste storage. 2: Minimal Storage:A small or inadequate area is used for health care waste storage, which may not meet safety standards. 3: Basic Storage:Some safe storage areas are available, but they may not be fully compliant with safety guidelines or large enough for all waste. 4: Moderate Storage:Waste is stored in a designated place, but improvements are needed for complete safety. 5: Full Storage:The facility has a designated, safe, and compliant storage area for health care waste, protecting it from contamination or unauthorised access.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.5. Does the health care facility have a safe waste disposal system before, during and after floods?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.5. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No System:There is no waste disposal system for flood events. 2: Minimal system:Waste is occasionally disposed of without following a formal plan during floods. 3: Basic System:A waste disposal system exists, but it is not comprehensive or consistently followed during floods. 4: Moderate System:The facility has a waste disposal system, but it needs improvements to function effectively during floods. 5: Full System:A fully operational and updated waste disposal system is in place and works effectively before, during, and after floods.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.6. Does the health care facility have an established safe management approach to health care waste transport (including hazardous waste) in case of floods?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.6. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no plan for safe health care waste transport during floods. 2: Minimal  plan:Some efforts are made to transport waste safely, but no formal approach exists. 3: Basic Plan:A plan exists, but it does not cover all hazardous waste or is not always followed during flood events. 4: Moderate Plan:The facility has a transport plan, but it requires updates or improvements to safely manage waste transport during floods. 5: Full Plan:A fully established and regularly updated approach to health care waste transport is in place, covering hazardous materials and flood scenarios.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.7. Does the health care facility have chemical, radioactive and biological hazardous waste stored in a safe place and on a level above the ground floor?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.7. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Safe Storage:Hazardous waste is not stored safely or on higher levels. 2: Minimal Safe Storage:Some hazardous waste is stored safely, but not all, and some remain on the ground floor. 3: Basic Safe Storage:Most hazardous waste is stored in safe locations above the ground floor, but not all materials are adequately protected. 4: Moderate Safe Storage:Hazardous waste is stored safely and above the ground floor, but improvements are needed for comprehensive protection. 5: Fully Safe Storage:All hazardous waste, including chemical, radioactive, and biological materials, is stored in a safe place on levels above the ground floor, protected from floods.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.8. Does the health care facility have water storage tanks appropriately covered to prevent access or contamination, and safely located for flooding events?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.8. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Cover or Location:Water storage tanks are neither covered nor placed in safe locations for flood protection. 2: Minimal Cover or location:Some tanks are covered, but they may not all be properly located to prevent flood-related contamination. 3: Basic Cover or Location:Tanks are covered and placed in relatively safe locations, but improvements are needed to ensure full protection. 4: Moderate Cover or Location in Place:Water storage tanks are covered and placed in safer areas, but they may not fully prevent contamination or be completely flood-safe. 5: Full Cover or Location in place:Water storage tanks are appropriately covered, and located in flood-safe areas to prevent access, contamination, or damage.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.9. Does the health care facility have onsite water purification equipment to provide safe drinking water?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.9. Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Equipment:The facility does not have any onsite water purification equipment 2: Minimal Equipment:Some basic water purification equipment exists, but it may not be adequate for emergency use or not fully functional. 3: Basic Equipment:Onsite water purification equipment is available, but its capacity is limited or not consistently maintained. 4: Moderate Equipment:Water purification equipment is onsite and functional but needs regular maintenance or upgrades to ensure readiness for emergencies. 5: Full Equipment:The facility has fully operational and regularly maintained onsite water purification equipment to ensure safe drinking water during emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.10 Does the health care facility have nonreturn valves installed on water supply pipes to prevent backflows?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.10 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Installation:The facility does not have non-return valves on its water supply pipes. 2: Minimal Installation:Some non-return valves are installed but not on all supply pipes, leaving areas vulnerable to backflows. 3: Basic Installation:Non-return valves are installed on most water supply pipes, but not all areas are protected from potential backflow. 4: Moderate Installation:Most water supply pipes have non-return valves, but a few remain without this protection, requiring further action. 5: Full Installation:All water supply pipes are equipped with non-return valves to effectively prevent backflows and safeguard the water supply system.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.11 Does the health care facilityhave waste pits able to withstand flood events?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.11 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Pits:There are no flood-resistant waste pits. 2: Minimal Pits:Some pits are designed to withstand floods, but others are vulnerable. 3: Basic Pits:Some flood-resistant waste pits are available, but they may not fully prevent flooding or are not comprehensively used. 4: Moderate Pits:The facility has waste pits that withstand flooding to some extent, but improvements are needed to ensure full protection. 5: Full Pits:All waste pits are designed and maintained to withstand floods, preventing contamination or overflow during flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.12 Does the health care facility have a surveillance system for diseases related to water quality and sanitation?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.12 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Surveillance:There is no system to monitor diseases related to water quality and sanitation. 2: Minimal Surveillance:Some surveillance is carried out, but it is not systematic or consistent. 3: Basic Surveillance:A surveillance system exists, but it is not fully operational or comprehensive. 4: Moderate Surveillance:A regular surveillance system exists but requires updates to cover all disease risks related to water quality and sanitation 5: Full Surveillance:The facility has a fully functional and regularly updated surveillance system for diseases linked to water quality and sanitation.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-rm-2.2.13 Does the health care facilitykeep waste sealed in rubbish bins to avoid rodents?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-rm-2.2.13 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:Waste is not kept sealed in bins, leading to rodent infestations. 2: Minimal Plan:Some waste is sealed in bins, but it is not consistently practiced throughout the facility. 3: Basic Plan:Waste is sealed in bins, but this is not fully enforced across all areas or for all types of waste. 4: Moderate Plan:Most waste is kept sealed in bins to avoid rodents, but the practice is not fully consistent across all areas or types of waste. 5: Full Plan:All waste is kept sealed in appropriate bins to avoid attracting rodents, and regular checks are in place to ensure compliance throughout the facility.

dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.1 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:The facility does not have an emergency water supply plan. 2: Minimal Plan:An emergency water supply plan is discussed but not formalised. 3: Basic Plan:The facility has a draft or partial emergency water supply plan, but it is not comprehensive or updated. 4: Moderate Plan:An emergency water supply plan exists but needs regular reviews and updates to remain effective. 5: Full Plan:The facility has a fully developed, comprehensive emergency water supply plan, regularly reviewed and updated for preparedness.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.2 Does the health care facility staff who are trained to an appropriate standard to maintain the correct level of safety of water quality controls, use of supplies and alternative sources?
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.2 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:Staff are not trained in water quality controls or use of alternative water sources 2: Minimal Training:Some staff have undergone basic training, but it is irregular and insufficient for emergencies. 3: Basic Training:Some staff are adequately trained, but the facility lacks a complete training programme for all relevant personnel. 4: Moderate Training:Most staff are trained in water quality safety controls, but the programme is not yet fully comprehensive or needs updates. 5: Full Training:The facility has a comprehensive and regularly updated training programme for all relevant staff on water quality safety controls and alternative sources.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.3 Does the health care facility have a water safety plan in place, in case of water contamination?*

dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no water safety plan for contamination scenarios. 2: Minimal Plan:A water safety plan is being discussed but has not yet been formalised or implemented. 3: Basic Plan:A water safety plan is in place, but it is not comprehensive or consistently followed during contamination incidents. 4: Moderate Plan:The facility has a water safety plan, but it requires updates or improvements to cover all possible contamination scenarios. 5: Full Plan:A fully developed, regularly updated water safety plan is in place, covering all contamination risks and providing clear protocols for emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.4 Does the health care facility have a mechanism or regulation to carry out sanitary inspections of water supply, and when necessary, establish a temporary ban on use, until improvements are made?

dataElements.description: 1: No Mechanism:There is no mechanism for conducting sanitary inspections or issuing temporary bans. 2: Minimal Mechanism:Sanitary inspections are conducted sporadically, but no formal mechanism exists for issuing temporary bans. 3: Basic Mechanism:A system for inspections and temporary bans is in place but lacks consistency or full implementation across all areas of the facility. 4: Moderate Mechanism:A formal mechanism exists for inspections and bans, but it requires improvements or updates to ensure effectiveness 5: Full Mechanism:A comprehensive and regularly updated mechanism is in place for carrying out sanitary inspections and imposing temporary bans on water use when necessary.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.5 Does the health care facility have a contingency plan to ensure effective and timely delivery of safe water during floods and emergencies over the short- and long-term?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.5 Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:The facility does not have a contingency plan for safe water delivery during floods and emergencies. 2: Minimal Plan:A contingency plan is being discussed but has not yet been formalised or implemented. 3: Basic Plan:A contingency plan exists, but it may not be comprehensive or fully operational during both short- and long-term emergencies. 4: Moderate Plan:A contingency plan is in place but requires updates or expansions to cover all scenarios over the short- and long-term. 5: Full Plan:A fully developed, regularly updated contingency plan is in place, ensuring safe water delivery during floods and emergencies over both timeframes.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.6 Does the health care facility have a plan to provide and maintain adequate cleaning and disinfection supplies (such as chlorine, filters or other water treatment technology, rapid water testing kit) for wate

dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in place:There is no plan for providing or maintaining cleaning and disinfection supplies for water safety. 2: Minimal Plan in place:Some supplies (e.g., chlorine or filters) are available, but no formal plan exists to ensure regular provision and maintenance. 3: Basic Plan in place:The facility has a plan, but it does not fully cover all necessary cleaning and disinfection supplies, or the plan is not consistently followed. 4: Moderate Plan in Place:The facility has a plan to maintain supplies, but it needs updates or expansions to cover all required items for water safety in emergencies. 5: Full Plan in Place:A fully operational and regularly updated plan is in place to ensure all necessary cleaning and disinfection supplies are available and maintained.

dataElements.name: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.7 Does the health care facility have an emergency plan for maintenance and restoration of waste management systems?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-wash-hsr-2.3.7 Does the health care facility
