dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.1 Does the health care facility Have knowledge, experience (considering previous damages) and resources (including human, material, financial, supplies chain and logistics) to manage flood risk reduction?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.1 Does the health care fac
dataElements.description: 1: No Knowledge, Experience, or Resources:No knowledge, experience, or resources are available to manage flood risk reduction. There is no preparedness or planning based on previous damages or potential risks. 2: Minimal Knowledge and Resources:Limited knowledge of flood risk reduction exists, with very little experience or resources available to manage flood risks. Lessons from previous damages are not fully considered, and available resources are insufficient for effective action. 3: Basic Knowledge and Resources:Some knowledge and experience in managing flood risks, including learning from past damages. Resources (human, material, and financial) are available but may be inadequate or inconsistent, limiting the ability to implement comprehensive flood risk reduction strategies 4: Moderately Adequate Knowledge, Experience, and Resources:Good level of knowledge, experience, and resources to manage flood risk reduction. Lessons from previous damages are incorporated into planning, and there are sufficient human, material, and financial resources, along with a functional supply chain and logistics, to manage flood risks effectively in most situations 5: Full/Comprehensive Knowledge, Experience, and Resources:Fully equipped with comprehensive knowledge, experience, and resources to manage flood risk reduction. All previous damages are thoroughly considered, and the organization is well-prepared with ample human, material, financial, and logistical resources, ensuring effective flood risk reduction and response capabilities.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.2 Does the health care facility Provide greater advocacy on health workforce education to cover climate change risks and responses?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.2 Does the health care fac
dataElements.description: 1: No Advocacy:There is no promotion or mention of the need to include climate change risks and responses in health workforce education. Climate change is considered irrelevant to health education. 2: Minimal Advocacy:There is a minimal acknowledgment that climate change might impact health, but no formal steps are taken to incorporate it into health workforce education. Discussions remain informal or occasional. 3: Basic Advocacy:Some advocacy efforts are made to raise awareness of the importance of climate change in health workforce education. However, these efforts are limited to specific initiatives or pilot projects rather than a broad, sustained push. 4: Moderate Advocacy:There is significant, organized effort to push for the integration of climate change risks and responses into health workforce education. Policy recommendations are made, and partnerships with educational institutions or stakeholders are formed to promote this cause. 5: Full Advocacy:There is a comprehensive, well-funded, and sustained campaign to prioritize climate change risks and responses in health workforce education. It is treated as a critical component of health training programs, with widespread support from both policymakers and educational institutions, and integrated into curricula at multiple levels.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.3 Does the health care facility Work with the local government to support vulnerable local populations to actively participate in risk reduction management, policy making, planning and implementation?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.3 Does the health care fac
dataElements.description: 1: No Collaboration:No collaboration exists with the local government to support vulnerable local populations in risk reduction management, policy making, planning, or implementation. 2: Minimal Collaboration:Minimal collaboration with the local government occurs, but there is little or no focus on actively involving vulnerable populations in risk reduction management, policy making, or planning. Efforts are limited or ad-hoc. 3: Basic Collaboration:Some collaboration with the local government exists to support vulnerable populations, but their participation in risk reduction management, policy making, and planning is limited. The efforts may be occasional, with only partial involvement of vulnerable communities. 4: Moderate Collaboration:Regular collaboration with the local government takes place, and there are established efforts to involve vulnerable populations in risk reduction management, policy making, and planning. While participation is encouraged, it may not be fully inclusive or consistent in all areas of implementation 5: Full Collaboration:Strong and consistent collaboration with the local government, with comprehensive efforts to ensure vulnerable populations actively participate in all aspects of risk reduction management, policy making, planning, and implementation. Their voices are integral to decision-making processes, ensuring inclusive and effective risk reduction strategies

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.4 Does the health care facility Conduct climate risk and vulnerability assessments for all facility sectors to identify risk scenarios, vulnerabilities and the facility’s response capacity?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.4 Does the health care fac
dataElements.description: 1: No Action:No climate risk and vulnerability assessments are conducted for any facility sectors. 2: Minimal Action:Conduct partial climate risk assessments for a limited number of facility sectors, identifying only major risk scenarios without detailed vulnerability analysis or response capacity evaluation. 3: Basic Action:Conduct climate risk assessments for some facility sectors, identifying key risk scenarios and basic vulnerabilities but with limited assessment of the facility’s overall response capacity. 4: Moderate Action:Conduct regular climate risk and vulnerability assessments for most facility sectors, thoroughly identifying risk scenarios, vulnerabilities, and evaluating the facility's response capacity across multiple dimensions. 5: Full Action:Conduct full-scale, integrated climate risk and vulnerability assessments for all facility sectors, identifying all potential risk scenarios, detailed vulnerabilities, and a deep analysis of the facility’s adaptive response capacity and resilience strategies.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.5 Does the health care facility Utilize the assessed information as a basis to plan and prioritize measures to reduce risk
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.5 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Utilization of Assessed Information:Does not utilize assessed information as a basis for planning or prioritizing measures to reduce risk. Decisions are made without any data or analysis. 2: Minimal Utilization:Assessed information is occasionally referenced, but it is not systematically used to plan or prioritize risk reduction measures. The approach is mostly reactive, with limited data-driven planning 3: Basic Utilization:Some assessed information is utilized to guide planning and prioritization of risk reduction measures, but it is inconsistently applied, and key data may be overlooked or underutilized in decision-making. 4: Moderate Utilization:Assessed information is regularly utilized as a basis for planning and prioritizing risk reduction measures. The process is generally data-driven, but there may be room for improvement in using the information more systematically or comprehensively. 5: Full Utilization:Fully utilizes assessed information in a systematic and comprehensive manner to plan and prioritize risk reduction measures. All relevant data is considered, and decisions are consistently based on thorough analysis, ensuring effective and strategic risk management.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.6 Does the health care facility In their annual planning consider how climate risks may change in the future?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.6 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No consideration:Does not consider how climate risks may change in the future during annual planning. 2: Minimal consideration:Barely acknowledges potential future climate risks but no formal action or planning is included. 3: Basic consideration:Considers some aspects of future climate risks in specific areas but lacks a comprehensive approach. 4: Moderate consideration:Consistently includes future climate risks in annual planning across various areas, though there are gaps in integration or application. 5: Full consideration:Fully integrates future climate risks into all aspects of annual planning with proactive strategies and contingency plans in place.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.7 Does the health care facility Have resources available to adopt risk reduction measures on the building and its infrastructure, technologies, products and processes?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.7 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Resources Available:No resources are available to adopt risk reduction measures for the building, infrastructure, technologies, products, or processes. No risk mitigation efforts are in place. 2: Minimal Resources:Very limited resources are available, allowing for minimal risk reduction measures to be applied. Only basic or temporary solutions can be implemented, with significant gaps in infrastructure and technological protection 3: Basic Resources:Some resources are available, but they are insufficient to fully address all areas. Risk reduction measures can be applied to certain parts of the building, infrastructure, or technologies, but not comprehensively 4: Moderately Adequate Resources:Resources are adequately available, allowing for risk reduction measures to be adopted across most aspects of the building, infrastructure, technologies, products, and processes. Most vulnerabilities are addressed, though some areas may still require additional attention. 5: Full/Comprehensive Resources:Fully equipped with ample resources to adopt comprehensive risk reduction measures for the building, infrastructure, technologies, products, and processes. All necessary improvements are implemented, ensuring maximum resilience and safety

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.8 Does the health care facility Regularly update these assessments, considering emerging scientific information?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.8 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No updates:No updates or revisions to the assessments are made, even when new scientific information becomes available. 2: Minimal Updates:updates to the assessments are infrequent and happen only when significant or unavoidable changes in scientific information occur. 3: Basic Updates:Assessments are updated from time to time, but only in response to major advances or when outdated information is evident. 4: Moderate Updates:Assessments are reviewed and updated regularly to incorporate new scientific findings, though there may be slight delays in responding to every new piece of information. 5: Full Updates:Assessments are constantly revised and adjusted in near real-time, ensuring they always reflect the latest scientific information available.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.9 Does the health care facility Have a schedule to inspect the facility regularly, both internally and externally, for signs of deterioration (e.g. cracks or sinking structural elements) to avoid or redu
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.9 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Inspection Schedule:No schedule exists to inspect the facility for signs of deterioration. There is no regular monitoring of structural elements, increasing vulnerability to flood impacts. 2: Minimal Inspection Schedule:A very basic or irregular inspection schedule is in place. Inspections may occur occasionally, but they are infrequent, and not all areas of the facility are covered. Potential risks from deterioration may go unnoticed. 3: Basic Inspection Schedule:An inspection schedule exists, but it is only partially implemented. Some areas of the facility are regularly inspected, but the schedule may lack consistency or thoroughness in detecting early signs of deterioration. 4: Moderately Adequate Inspection Schedule:A regular and consistent inspection schedule is in place, covering both internal and external areas of the facility. Most signs of deterioration, such as cracks or sinking structural elements, are identified and addressed to reduce flood impacts. 5: Full/Comprehensive Inspection Schedule:Fully equipped with a comprehensive and systematic schedule for inspecting the facility internally and externally. All potential signs of deterioration are regularly monitored, and timely maintenance or repairs are carried out to ensure maximum protection from flood impacts.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.10 Does the health care facility Evaluate the condition and safety of structural and nonstructural elements impacted by previous exposure to flood?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.10 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No evaluation:no eveluation of the condition or safety of structural and nonstructural elements impacted by previous exposure to flood. 2: Minimal evaluation:Basic visual inspection of structural and nonstructural elements to identify obvious signs of damage caused by flood exposure. 3: Basic evaluation:Detailed assessment of structural and nonstructural elements, including documentation of visible damage, but limited to surface-level inspections without specialized tools or techniques. 4: Moderate evaluation:Comprehensive evaluation involving both visual inspections and non-invasive testing methods (e.g., moisture sensors, infrared imaging) to assess the condition and safety of structural and nonstructural elements impacted by flooding. 5: Full evaluation:Thorough, in-depth evaluation using advanced diagnostic tools, expert analysis, and structural testing (e.g., core sampling, load testing) to determine the integrity and safety of structural and nonstructural elements after exposure to flood, with recommendations for remediation if necessary.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.11 Does the health care facility Have a safe location for critical services and equipment in a flood emergency situation
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.11 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Safe Location:No safe location exists for critical services and equipment during a flood emergency. Equipment and services are at high risk of being damaged or compromised. 2: Minimal Safe Location:A temporary or inadequate safe location is available for only some critical services or equipment. There is limited protection, and many essential items may still be at risk during a flood. 3: Basic Safe Location:A designated safe location is in place for some critical services and equipment, but not all. Protection is partially ensured, but some essential services or equipment may still be exposed to flood risks. 4: Moderately Adequate Safe Location:A secure and appropriate safe location exists for most critical services and equipment during a flood emergency. There is a good level of protection, with only minor gaps in ensuring full safety 5: Full/Comprehensive Safe Location:Fully equipped with a well-protected and secure safe location for all critical services and equipment during a flood emergency. Complete protection is ensured, minimizing the risk of damage or disruption.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.12 Does the health care facility Have a safety plan to prevent medical and laboratorial equipment and supplies, and food packages to be exposed to flood waters?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.12 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Safety Plan:No safety plan exists to prevent medical and laboratory equipment, supplies, or food packages from being exposed to floodwaters. Items are highly vulnerable to flood damage. 2: Minimal Safety Plan:A very basic or limited safety plan is in place, but it only covers some items or areas. Medical and laboratory equipment, supplies, or food packages remain largely at risk of exposure to floodwaters 3: Basic Safety Plan:A safety plan exists, but it is not comprehensive. Some medical and laboratory equipment, supplies, and food packages are protected, but significant gaps remain, leaving some items exposed to flood risks. 4: Moderately Adequate Safety Plan:A solid safety plan is in place, providing protection for most medical and laboratory equipment, supplies, and food packages. The plan addresses potential flood exposure for these items effectively, with minor areas for improvement. 5: Full/Comprehensive Safety Plan:A fully developed and comprehensive safety plan is in place to prevent all medical and laboratory equipment, supplies, and food packages from being exposed to floodwaters. The plan is thorough and ensures maximum protection, leaving no items vulnerable to flood risks.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.13 Does the health care facility Have procedures to store food and bottled water on shelves that will be safely out of the way of contaminated water in case of flooding?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.13 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No preparedness:No procedures in place to store food and bottled water safely during a flood. Items are left in areas prone to contamination. 2: Minimal preparedness:A general awareness that food and water should be stored safely, but no specific procedures or storage systems are established. Food and water might be on low shelves or in areas vulnerable to flooding. 3: Basic preparedness:Some procedures exist, such as keeping food and water in sealed containers. However, storage might still be in areas where floodwater could reach, like on low shelves, but they’re somewhat elevated from the floor. 4: Moderate preparedness:Procedures are well-defined, and food and bottled water are stored on shelves that are explicitly placed above potential flood levels. However, the system may lack redundancy or thorough checks to ensure safety in more extreme conditions. 5: Full preparedness:Comprehensive procedures are in place, including storing food and water in flood-proof areas, like high shelves or sealed cabinets specifically designed to prevent contamination. Regular checks and drills ensure that the system functions effectively even in worst-case flood scenarios.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.14 Does the health care facility Have an effective emergency risk communication plan to reduce risks and impacts for health workers and patients?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.14 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Communication Plan:No emergency risk communication plan exists. Health workers and patients are left uninformed or unprepared in emergency situations, increasing risks and impacts. 2: Minimal Communication Plan:A very basic or informal communication plan exists, but it is not well-structured or consistently followed. Only a limited number of health workers and patients receive essential information, leaving gaps in preparedness and response. 3: Basic Communication Plan:A communication plan is in place, but it only partially addresses the needs of health workers and patients. Some risk information is conveyed, but the plan lacks comprehensive coverage and timeliness, reducing its overall effectiveness. 4: Moderately Adequate Communication Plan:An effective communication plan is established, with most health workers and patients receiving timely and relevant information to reduce risks and impacts. The plan functions well but may have some areas for improvement in reaching all target audiences or handling specific emergencies. 5: Full/Comprehensive Communication Plan:A fully developed and highly effective emergency risk communication plan is in place. All health workers and patients receive timely, accurate, and clear information, significantly reducing risks and impacts. The plan is well-coordinated, comprehensive, and responsive to any emergency situation

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.15 Does the health care facility Have a contingency plan in place for safe and efficient personnel evacuation (including health staff and patients) before, during and following a flood?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.15 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Contingency Plan:No contingency plan exists for the safe and efficient evacuation of personnel. Health staff and patients are left vulnerable without clear guidance or procedures during a flood 2: Minimal Contingency Plan:A basic or informal evacuation plan is in place, but it lacks structure, coordination, and comprehensiveness. Only some personnel, health staff, or patients may be evacuated safely, with many gaps in the process 3: Basic Contingency Plan:A contingency plan exists, but it is limited in scope and efficiency. Some aspects of safe evacuation are addressed, but the plan may not cover all personnel or be effective at all stages (before, during, and after a flood). Coordination and preparedness are partial 4: Moderately Adequate Contingency Plan:An effective and well-structured contingency plan is in place, covering most personnel, health staff, and patients. The plan ensures relatively safe and efficient evacuation, addressing key stages of the flood emergency. While functional, there may be minor areas for improvement in coordination or comprehensiveness 5: Full/Comprehensive Contingency Plan:A fully developed and highly efficient contingency plan is in place, ensuring the safe and coordinated evacuation of all personnel, health staff, and patients before, during, and following a flood. The plan is comprehensive, well-coordinated, and adaptable to different flood scenarios, ensuring minimal risk to all involved.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.16 Does the health care facility Have a clear and consistent mechanism for secure evacuation of health workers and patients?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.16 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Evacuation Mechanism:No mechanism exists for the secure evacuation of health workers and patients. There is no clear guidance or process in place, leaving people vulnerable in an emergency situation 2: Minimal Evacuation Mechanism:A basic or informal evacuation mechanism exists, but it is not well-structured, and only some aspects of the evacuation process are clear. The mechanism may not ensure the full security of all health workers and patients." 3: Basic Mechanism:A mechanism is in place, but it is only partially developed and inconsistently applied. Some health workers and patients may be securely evacuated, but the process may lack clarity, coordination, or consistency, potentially leaving gaps in security 4: Moderately Evacuation Mechanism:A clear and consistent evacuation mechanism is in place for most health workers and patients. The process is generally secure and well-coordinated, though there may be minor improvements needed to ensure full security or adaptability in various emergency scenarios. 5: Full/Comprehensive Evacuation Mechanism:"A fully developed, clear, and consistent mechanism is in place for the secure evacuation of all health workers and patients. The process is highly organized, ensuring maximum security and coordination across all stages of evacuation in any emergency situation.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.17 Does the health care facility Have a plan to transfer critical equipment and medical supplies to another health care facility or to a secure storage?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.17 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Transfer Plan:No plan exists for transferring critical equipment and medical supplies. In the event of an emergency, equipment and supplies are left vulnerable to damage or loss. 2: Minimal Transfer Plan:A basic or informal transfer plan exists, but it is incomplete or lacks proper coordination. Only some critical equipment or supplies may be transferred in an emergency, with significant gaps in security or preparedness. 3: Basic Transfer Plan:A plan is in place to transfer critical equipment and medical supplies, but it is only partially developed. Some items can be moved to another facility or secure storage, but the plan may not cover all scenarios or provide efficient processes for quick action. 4: Moderately Adequate Transfer Plan:A clear and well-organized plan exists to transfer most critical equipment and medical supplies. The process is secure and generally effective, though there may be room for improvement in fully securing all items or enhancing response speed during emergencies 5: Fully/Comprehensive Transfer Plan:A fully developed and comprehensive plan is in place to transfer all critical equipment and medical supplies to another healthcare facility or secure storage. The plan is well-coordinated, efficient, and adaptable to various emergency situations, ensuring maximum protection for all assets.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.18 Does the health care facility Have evaluation tools (e.g. forms) to identify damages and minimum needs in terms of health workers and medical supplies to ensure continuous functioning of services?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.18 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No evaluation tools:No evaluation tools, forms, or systems in place to assess damages, medical staff needs, or medical supply shortages during or after emergencies. 2: Minimal evaluation tools:Informal or ad-hoc methods are used to evaluate damages and needs, but no standardized forms or structured procedures are established. The process relies on individual knowledge and initiative without clear guidelines. 3: Basic evaluation tools:Basic evaluation forms exist but are generic and not tailored for specific situations. They may capture basic data on damages, staffing, and medical supplies, but lack detailed sections for comprehensive assessment and action planning. 4: Moderate evaluation tools:Standardized evaluation forms and tools are in place, designed to assess specific damage types and minimum needs for health workers and supplies. These tools are used in regular assessments, but may not cover all possible emergency scenarios or allow for rapid scaling during crises. 5: Full/Comprehensive evaluation tool:Well-developed, situation-specific evaluation tools and forms are readily available. They are designed to assess damages comprehensively, identify exact medical staff and supply shortages, and ensure the continuous functioning of services. Regular updates, staff training, and practice drills ensure that these tools are effectively implemented during emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.19 Does the health care facility Have a mechanism for providing prompt maintenance and repair of equipment required for essential services?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.19 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No mechanism:No system or process in place for the maintenance or repair of equipment essential for services. Equipment failures cause significant disruptions, with no plan for timely intervention. 2: Minimal mechanism:Minimal awareness exists about the need for equipment maintenance, but there is no formal mechanism. Repairs are handled on a reactive basis when equipment fails, leading to delayed response times and disruptions in essential services. 3: Basic mechanism:A system is in place to address basic equipment maintenance and repair, but it is limited in scope. Scheduled maintenance is occasional, and repairs are only addressed as they arise. The response to breakdowns is slow, and the mechanism lacks proactive planning. 4: Moderate mechanism:A structured mechanism exists for regular maintenance and prompt repair of essential equipment. There are predefined procedures for both preventive maintenance and emergency repairs. Resources are allocated, but there may be occasional delays or gaps in response during high-demand situations. 5: Full/Effective mechanism:A comprehensive and proactive mechanism is in place, ensuring regular maintenance and immediate repair of equipment critical to essential services. The system includes real-time monitoring, rapid response teams, and a well-stocked inventory of spare parts. This ensures minimal downtime and uninterrupted operation of essential services, even during emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20 Does the health care facility Have a plan for relocating medical devices, medicines, mobile equipment and other supplies and services in case of operational disruption or outbreaks and epidemics that o
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No plan:There is no plan in place to relocate medical devices, medicines, mobile equipment, or supplies during operational disruptions or outbreaks. The facility is highly vulnerable to interruptions, with no clear strategy for maintaining service continuity. 2: Minimal plan:There is a basic awareness of the need to relocate critical items, but no formal plan or strategy exists. Any relocation is done in an ad-hoc manner during disruptions, resulting in confusion and inefficiency, with high risk of service breakdown. 3: Basic plan:A basic plan exists, outlining the relocation of key medical devices, medicines, and supplies in case of disruptions. However, the plan lacks detail or flexibility and may not cover all potential scenarios. It might work for small-scale incidents but could be overwhelmed during large-scale outbreaks or major operational disruptions. 4: Moderate plan:A well-defined relocation plan is in place, specifying which equipment, medicines, and supplies need to be moved and where they should be relocated during disruptions. The plan includes assigned roles and procedures, with periodic drills or evaluations to ensure it works. It may have some gaps in coordination or scalability during large epidemics. 5: Full/Comprehensive plan:A highly detailed and comprehensive plan is in place, covering all scenarios of operational disruption, outbreaks, or epidemics that could overwhelm the facility. It includes relocation strategies for all critical medical devices, medicines, and equipment, with pre-identified alternate locations, transport arrangements, and staff assignments. Regular drills, updates, and cross-facility coordination ensure that services continue with minimal disruption.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20-a Does the health care facility Assess the performance and vulnerabilities of each critical part of the facility (structural and nonstructural elements) that can be affected by floods?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20-a Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No assessment:No assessments have been conducted to evaluate the performance or vulnerabilities of the facility’s structural and nonstructural elements in case of floods. The facility has no data or plan in place to mitigate flood risks. 2: Minimal assessment:There is limited awareness of potential flood risks, but no formal assessment of the facility’s vulnerabilities. Any evaluation is informal and non-structured, relying on visual inspections without professional analysis or data. Only obvious vulnerabilities are recognized. 3: Basic assessment:A basic assessment has been conducted, identifying some critical structural elements (e.g., foundations, walls) that may be vulnerable to floods. Nonstructural elements (e.g., electrical systems, equipment) are only partially considered. The assessment may lack detail, with no formal reporting or prioritization of mitigation measures. 4: Moderate assessment:A moderate assessment has been performed, covering most critical structural (e.g., building integrity, drainage systems) and nonstructural elements (e.g., HVAC, medical equipment) of the facility. Potential vulnerabilities during floods have been identified, and mitigation measures are outlined. However, some areas may lack comprehensive risk analysis, and implementation of measures may be limited. 5: Full/Comprehensive assessment:A thorough and detailed assessment has been conducted for all critical structural (e.g., building foundations, roofing) and nonstructural elements (e.g., utilities, emergency systems, medical equipment) in the facility. Flood performance and vulnerabilities have been identified using professional tools, models, and standards. The assessment includes prioritized mitigation strategies, clear action plans, and regular updates to ensure continuous improvement of flood resilience.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.21 Does the health care facility Have measures to remove mosquito breeding sites?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.21 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No measures:No measures in place to identify or remove mosquito breeding sites. No actions are taken, and breeding sites may be widespread, leading to a high risk of mosquito-borne diseases. 2: Minimal measures:Minimal awareness of mosquito breeding sites, but minimal actions are taken. Some occasional efforts, such as removing standing water, may be made, but there is no formalized or regular process for addressing the issue. 3: Basic measures:Basic measures are in place, such as periodic inspections and efforts to remove common breeding sites like standing water. However, these actions are irregular and reactive, without a consistent or coordinated plan. Some breeding sites may still go unnoticed or untreated. 4: Moderate measures:A structured plan exists for identifying and removing mosquito breeding sites, with regular inspections of areas prone to standing water (e.g., drains, containers). The plan is moderately enforced, and efforts are made to educate staff or the public about preventing mosquito breeding. There may be some gaps in coverage or execution. 5: Full/Comprehensive measures:A comprehensive, well-coordinated program is in place to regularly identify and eliminate mosquito breeding sites. This includes frequent inspections, rapid removal of standing water, use of mosquito control agents (if necessary), and public education campaigns. The plan is updated regularly based on environmental changes, and all areas are systematically covered to ensure minimal mosquito-breeding risks.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.22 Does the health care facility Have rooftop structures and equipment revised for anticipated increased rainfall?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.22 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No revision:No assessment or revisions for increased rainfall; high vulnerability. 2: Minimal revision:Basic awareness but no formal revisions; occasional minor adjustments like clearing drains. 3: Basic revision:Basic adjustments made, but limited scope; larger vulnerabilities may not be addressed. 4: Moderate revision:Key revisions made (roof materials, equipment protection); lacks long-term upgrades or detailed weatherproofing. 5: Full/Comprehensive revision:Thorough, ongoing revisions with reinforced roofs, upgraded drainage, and weatherproof equipment, ready for severe rainfall.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.23 Does the health care facility Have roofs that are leak-proof and insulated?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.23 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No protection:Roofs are neither leak-proof nor insulated, making them highly vulnerable to leaks and temperature fluctuations. 2: Minimal protection:Some awareness of the need for leak-proof and insulated roofs, but no formal action. Roofs may have minor fixes, but leaks and poor insulation remain a problem. 3: Basic protection:Basic leak-proofing and insulation efforts have been made, but they may not be fully effective. Small leaks may still occur, and insulation might be incomplete or inadequate. 4: Moderate protection:The roofs are mostly leak-proof and have decent insulation, though some areas may still be vulnerable during extreme weather. The system works well under normal conditions but might require periodic maintenance. 5: Full/Comprehensive protection:Roofs are fully leak-proof and well-insulated, with high-quality materials and construction. They are regularly inspected and maintained, ensuring protection from leaks and effective insulation in all weather conditions.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.24 Does the health care facility Have machine rooms that are resistant to flooding or rooftop damage?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.24 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No protection:Machine rooms have no flood resistance or protection from rooftop damage, leaving equipment highly vulnerable to water or debris. 2: Minimal protection:Basic awareness exists, but there are no formal measures in place. Some minimal actions may be taken, like elevating equipment, but rooms remain largely exposed to flooding or roof damage. 3: Basic protection:Some flood-resistant measures are in place, such as partial elevation or barriers, but protection is limited. Rooftop damage could still lead to significant issues in machine rooms. 4: Moderate protection:Machine rooms have been moderately protected against flooding and rooftop damage. Barriers, drainage systems, and reinforced structures are installed, but there may be occasional vulnerabilities in extreme conditions. 5: Full/Comprehensive protection:Machine rooms are fully resistant to flooding and rooftop damage. They are built with flood-proof designs, elevated platforms, reinforced walls, and roofing systems. Regular inspections and maintenance ensure continuous resilience against extreme weather conditions or rooftop failures.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.25 Does the health care facility Have a coordinated mechanism across the health sector in different levels of government, to manage the response and risks resulting from public health emergencies and disa
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.25 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No coordinationated mechanism:No coordinated mechanism exists. Each level of government operates independently, with no sharing of resources, patient transfers, or workforce support during emergencies. 2: Minimal coordinated mechanism:Limited awareness of the need for coordination, but no formal structure is in place. Some ad-hoc resource sharing may occur, but there is no systematic collaboration between different levels of government. 3: Basic coordinated mechanism:A basic mechanism exists, with some communication between levels of government. There is occasional resource sharing and patient transfers, but the process is informal and lacks efficiency during larger-scale emergencies. 4: Moderate coordinated mechanism:A structured mechanism is in place for managing responses, sharing resources, and transferring patients. The collaboration between different levels of government is active, but there may still be gaps in efficiency or scalability during major disasters. 5: Full/Comprehensive coordination:A fully coordinated mechanism is well-established, with seamless collaboration across all levels of government. Resources, supplies, patient transfers, and health workforce support are efficiently managed. Regular drills and updates ensure the system functions effectively in any public health emergency or disaster.

dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.26 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No procedures:No procedures are in place for procuring, transporting, or storing essential medical supplies. There is a high risk of supply chain disruptions and unsafe handling. 2: Minimal procedures:Basic awareness exists, but no formal procedures are established. Safe transport may be partially addressed, but the system lacks thorough checks, monitoring, and consistent application for all medical supplies. 4: Moderate procedures:Well-established procedures exist for safely procuring, transporting, and storing medical devices, pharmaceuticals, vaccines, and other essential supplies. The system works effectively, but there may be occasional gaps in monitoring or emergency preparedness. 5: Full/Comprehensive procedures:Fully developed and rigorously followed procedures ensure the safe procurement, transport, and storage of all critical medical supplies. The process is highly organized, monitored, and responsive, ensuring continuity and safety even in emergencies or during high demand. Regular audits and updates keep the system efficient and secure.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.27 Does the health care facility Have a plan to house staff at the health care facility if shelter is required (sleeping areas, food, water)?

dataElements.description: 1: No plan:No plan exists to house staff at the healthcare facility in case of emergency. There are no designated sleeping areas, or provisions for food and water. 2: Minimal plan:Minimal awareness, but no formal plan. Some informal arrangements may be made during emergencies, but staff housing lacks clear organization and provisions are inconsistent. 3: Basic plan:A basic plan is in place, with designated areas for staff to sleep and minimal provisions for food and water. However, the plan is not comprehensive, and resources may be inadequate for long-term shelter needs. 4: Moderate plan:A well-structured plan exists, with dedicated sleeping areas and sufficient food, water, and basic necessities for staff. The plan can manage short-term shelter needs and is generally effective, but may need improvements for longer durations or larger-scale emergencies. 5: Full/Comprehensive plan:A fully developed and robust plan is in place, with ample sleeping areas, food, water, and other necessities. The facility can comfortably house staff for extended periods during emergencies. The plan is regularly updated, includes contingency measures, and ensures staff well-being throughout the crisis.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-asci-4.1.28 Does the health care facility Have an established postflood recovery plan of all infrastructure facilities (structural and nonstructural elements)?*

There are no protocols for assessing damage or restoring services after a flood. 2: Minimal plan:Basic awareness of the need for a recovery plan exists, but no formal structure is in place. Some informal efforts may occur after a flood, but they lack coordination and are insufficient for comprehensive recovery. 3: Basic plan:A basic post-flood recovery plan is established, addressing some key infrastructure elements. The plan includes preliminary damage assessment and restoration actions but may not cover all facilities or be comprehensive in scope. 4: Moderate plan:A well-structured post-flood recovery plan exists, encompassing both structural and nonstructural elements. The plan includes detailed procedures for damage assessment, recovery priorities, and restoration timelines, but may still have gaps in resources or coordination. 5: Full/Comprehensive plan:A comprehensive and thoroughly developed post-flood recovery plan is in place for all infrastructure facilities. It includes detailed assessments, prioritized recovery actions, resource allocation, and ongoing evaluations. The plan is regularly updated and incorporates lessons learned from past events to enhance resilience and response efficiency.

Some data and alerts regarding climate hazards are communicated between the health sector and meteorological services, but the system lacks efficiency, regular updates, and comprehensive coverage 4: Moderately Adequate Information System:A well-established information system exists that facilitates regular communication between the health sector and meteorological services. However, it is not comprehensive, lacks detail, and is not regularly reviewed. 4: Moderate Plan:A moderately comprehensive plan is in place, addressing various aspects of flood-related risks at the facility's location. The plan is reviewed periodically for updates. 5: Full Plan:A fully established plan systematically addresses all climate risks related to floods, covering all relevant aspects. The plan is integrated into the facility's operations, with regular updates and improvements.

The plan is fully integrated into supply chain management practices.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.4 Does the health care facility Have electronic patient health records to make available to other receiving health care facilities, in case of evacuation?

dataElements.description: 1: No Electronic Patient Health Records:There are no electronic patient health records in place. Patient information is either not recorded or only available in paper format, making it impossible to share with other healthcare facilities during an evacuation 2: Minimal Electronic Patient Health Records:Some basic electronic patient health records exist, but they are not consistently maintained or accessible. Limited patient information may be available, but sharing with other healthcare facilities during an evacuation is cumbersome and unreliable 3: Basic Electronic Patient Health Records:Electronic patient health records are in place, but they are not fully integrated or comprehensive. While some patient data can be shared with other healthcare facilities, the system lacks efficiency and may miss critical information during an evacuation. 4: Moderately Adequate Electronic Patient Health Records:"An adequate system of electronic patient health records exists, allowing for regular sharing of patient information with receiving healthcare facilities during evacuations. Most essential data is accessible, but there may still be some limitations in the comprehensiveness or speed of data transfer 5: Full/Comprehensive Electronic Patient Health Records:"A fully integrated and comprehensive electronic patient health record system is in place. This system allows for seamless and instant sharing of complete patient information with other healthcare facilities during an evacuation, ensuring continuity of care and quick access to critical health data.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.5 Does the health care facility Have information and communication systems safely secured with backup arrangements (via cloud, satellite) to satisfy the facility’s demand?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.5 Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Backup Systems:"There are no backup systems in place for information and communication. All data and communication systems are vulnerable to disruptions, and there is no ability to recover data or maintain communication in case of a system failure. 2: Minimal Backup Systems:"Basic or informal backup systems exist, but they are poorly maintained or unreliable. Some data may be backed up, but the backup arrangements (e.g., cloud or satellite) are insufficient to meet the facility’s demands during an emergency. 3: Basic Backup Systems:Backup systems, including cloud or satellite, are in place but not fully secured or optimized. They can handle a portion of the facility's demand, but the system may not be able to fully support critical communication and data recovery during an extended outage or emergency situation. 4: Moderately Adequate Backup Systems:nformation and communication systems are adequately secured with reliable backup arrangements, including cloud and satellite options. These systems can generally meet the facility's demand in emergencies, though there may be occasional limitations in capacity or speed 5: Full/Comprehensive and Secure Backup Systems:"Fully secured information and communication systems are in place with robust backup arrangements via cloud and satellite. These systems are highly reliable, meeting all the facility's demands even during extended emergencies or large-scale outages, ensuring uninterrupted communication and data protection

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.6 Does the health care facility Ensure information and communication flow between the health workforce and policy makers, particularly during high-stress situations and demands created by emergencies?

dataElements.description: 1: No infromation & communication flow:There is no established communication process to ensure information flow between the health workforce and policymakers during emergencies. 2: Minimal infromation & communication flow:Communication channels are very limited and often used informally or sporadically during emergencies. There is no consistency in the information flow. 3: Basic infromation & communication flow:Basic communication structures exist, with some guidelines for managing information flow during emergencies. However, the procedures may not cover all scenarios or be consistently applied. 4: Moderate infromation & communication flow:Moderately comprehensive communication protocols are in place, with well-defined roles and channels to ensure effective information flow during high-stress situations. The protocols are regularly reviewed. 5: Full infromation & communication flow:Fully developed communication protocols exist, ensuring consistent, timely, and effective information flow between the health workforce and policymakers. The protocols are well-established and function effectively during all emergencies.

They may not address all scenarios effectively. 4: Moderate knoweldge transfer procedure:The facility has moderately consistent knowledge transfer procedures, with well-defined steps for sharing information during public health emergencies. The procedures are reviewed periodically. 5: Full knoweldge transfer procedure:A comprehensive, standardized knowledge transfer procedure is in place, ensuring that information is effectively shared across the organization during public health emergencies. The procedures are consistent and regularly updated.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.8 Does the health care facility Have identified capacities, resources and needs to better cope and manage floods?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.8 Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Identification of Capacities, Resources, or Needs:There is no identification of capacities, resources, or needs related to flood management. The facility is unprepared and unable to effectively cope with or respond to flood events. 2: Minimal Identification of Capacities and Resources:"Some basic understanding of the facility’s capacities and resources exists, but there is no comprehensive identification of needs. The facility has limited capability to cope with or manage floods, leaving significant gaps in its preparedness and response. 3: Basic Identification of Capacities, Resources, and Needs:"Capacities and resources have been partially identified, along with some needs, but the analysis is incomplete. The facility has moderate abilities to manage floods, but there are still some critical gaps in its understanding of what is required for full preparedness. 4: Moderately Adequate Identification of Capacities, Resources, and Needs:"Adequate identification of capacities, resources, and needs has been conducted. The facility is well-prepared to manage floods and can cope with most flood-related challenges. However, there may still be a few areas that require further refinement or additional resources. 5: Full/Comprehensive Identification of Capacities, Resources, and Needs:"A fully comprehensive analysis has been conducted, with all capacities, resources, and needs clearly identified. The facility is highly prepared and equipped to cope with and manage floods, with no significant gaps in its flood preparedness and response strategy

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.9 Does the health care facility Perform site and building maintenance procedures that include specifications on how the weather may affect the safety and continued functioning of the facility?

dataElements.description: 1: No maintenance:There are no maintenance procedures that specifically address weather-related safety for the site and buildings. 2: Minimal Maintenace:Maintenance procedures include very few weather-related considerations, often applied inconsistently or only in response to specific incidents. 3: Basic Maintenace:Basic maintenance procedures exist with some weather-related safety considerations, but they may not be detailed or cover all relevant aspects. 4: Moderate Maintenace:Moderately detailed maintenance procedures are in place, with specifications on weather-related safety. These procedures are regularly reviewed and updated to account for weather impacts. 5: Full Maintenance:The facility has comprehensive maintenance procedures that fully address weather-related safety. The procedures are well-documented, consistently applied, and integrated into the facility's overall maintenance plan, with regular updates.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-pst-4.2.10 Does the health care facility Have an information system for tracking and monitoring diseases following flood events?

dataElements.description: 1: No Information System:"There is no information system in place for tracking or monitoring diseases after flood events. Some data may be tracked, but the system lacks comprehensive coverage and is insufficient for effectively monitoring disease outbreaks after floods. 3: Basic Information System:"An information system is in place for tracking and monitoring diseases, but it is limited in scope and functionality. It can handle some disease tracking following flood events, but the system lacks full integration and may miss critical data, limiting its overall effectiveness. 4: Moderately Adequate Information System:"An adequate and structured information system is established for tracking and monitoring diseases after floods. It is functional and covers most disease surveillance needs, allowing for timely data collection and analysis, although minor gaps or delays may still occur." 5: Full/Comprehensive Information System:"A fully integrated and highly reliable information system is in place, capable of tracking and monitoring diseases comprehensively following flood events. It ensures real-time data collection, analysis, and reporting, providing health authorities with critical information to respond quickly and effectively to disease outbreaks

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.1 Does the health care facility have adaptive governance capacity regarding evaluation and measures for risk identification, risk reduction and response?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.1 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No adaptive governance capacity:No mechanisms or structures for risk identification, risk reduction, or response. Lack of awareness, policies, or strategies to evaluate or address risks. Governance is entirely reactive, with no proactive evaluation or planning. 2: Minimal adaptive governance capacity:Basic awareness of the need for risk identification and reduction, but actions are ad hoc and not systematized. Risk response mechanisms exist but are poorly coordinated and reactive. Evaluation processes may be informal or inconsistent, with limited feedback into decision-making. 3: Basic adaptive governance capacity:Initial systems for risk identification and evaluation are in place, though they may not be comprehensive or integrated. Some risk reduction and response measures are developed but are not consistently applied or evaluated. Evaluation mechanisms are starting to inform policy decisions, but they are not yet robust or fully integrated into governance structures. 4: Moderate adaptive governance capacity:Structured and systematic processes for risk identification, risk reduction, and response are established and actively used. Evaluation frameworks are applied consistently, and they contribute to iterative learning and adjustments. Governance systems are adaptable, responding effectively to evolving risks, though there may still be room for improvement in coordination or scale. 5: Full/High adaptive governance capacity:Comprehensive, proactive, and integrated systems for risk identification, reduction, and response are fully operational. Continuous evaluation processes are embedded in governance, leading to real-time adjustments and a high degree of adaptability. Risk management and governance are highly responsive, anticipating future risks and evolving proactively to mitigate and respond to them.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.2 Does the health care facility Have partnerships established between the facility, community and local authorities to reduce vulnerabilities in the surrounding areas?

dataElements.description: 1: No Partnerships Established:No partnerships exist between the facility, the community, and local authorities. There is no collaboration to reduce vulnerabilities, leaving the surrounding areas highly exposed to risks 2: Minimal Partnerships:Very basic or informal partnerships are in place, but they are weak and lack structure. Collaboration between the facility, community, and local authorities is minimal, leading to limited impact in reducing vulnerabilities in the surrounding areas 3: Basic Partnerships:Some partnerships exist between the facility, the community, and local authorities, but they are not fully developed. While there is some level of collaboration, it is inconsistent or only addresses certain vulnerabilities, leaving gaps in risk reduction efforts 4: Moderately Adequate Partnerships:Strong partnerships are established between the facility, community, and local authorities. Collaboration is well-coordinated and effective in reducing vulnerabilities in most areas, though some aspects of the partnership may need further strengthening or expansion. 5: Full/Comprehensive Partnerships:Fully developed and active partnerships exist between the facility, community, and local authorities. These partnerships are highly effective in reducing vulnerabilities across the surrounding areas, ensuring comprehensive and coordinated efforts to address risks.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.3 Does the health care facility Have a secure storage for hazardous chemicals to avoid their damage or release during a flood event?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.3 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Secure Storage:No secure storage exists for hazardous chemicals. These chemicals are at high risk of being damaged or released during a flood event, posing significant health and environmental risks 2: Minimal Secure Storage:A basic or poorly secured storage area exists for hazardous chemicals, but it offers limited protection. There is a high likelihood that chemicals could be damaged or released during a flood. 3: Basic Secure Storage:Some level of secure storage is in place for hazardous chemicals, providing partial protection. However, the storage may not fully meet safety standards, and there is still a moderate risk of damage or release during a flood. 4: Moderately Adequate Secure Storage:An adequately secure storage system is in place for hazardous chemicals, offering solid protection during flood events. Most chemicals are well-protected, but there may be minor improvements needed to ensure complete safety. 5: Full/Comprehensive Secure Storage:A fully secure and flood-resistant storage system is in place for all hazardous chemicals. The storage is highly effective at preventing damage or release, providing maximum protection during flood events.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-#4.3.4 Does the health care facility Have a defined and sustained budget as part of core budgeting for emergency preparedness and response to flood events?*
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-#4.3.4 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Defined Budget:No defined budget exists for emergency preparedness and response to flood events. Financial resources are not allocated, leaving the facility unprepared in case of a flood emergency. 2: Minimal Budget Allocation:A minimal or ad-hoc budget is occasionally allocated for emergency preparedness, but it is not part of the core budget. Financial resources may be insufficient, and the response to flood events is limited 3: Basic Budget:A defined budget exists, but it is small or inconsistent. Some resources are available for emergency preparedness and response to flood events, but they may be insufficient to fully meet the needs during an emergency 4: Moderately Adequate Budget:A defined and adequate budget is established as part of the core budgeting process for emergency preparedness and flood response. Most needs are covered, but the funding may not be fully sustained or adaptable for large-scale events 5: Full/Comprehensive and Sustained Budget:A fully defined and sustained budget is consistently included in the core budgeting for emergency preparedness and response to flood events. This ensures full financial readiness to meet all necessary actions and adaptations for flood emergencies.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.5 Does the health care facility Have an access route for public transportation which is likely to remain operational during or immediately following a flood event?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.5 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No Access Route:There is no designated access route for public transportation that can remain operational during or after a flood. Transportation is highly vulnerable and will likely be completely disrupted during a flood event. 2: Minimal Access Route:There is an informal or poorly maintained access route for public transportation, but it is unlikely to remain operational during or after a flood event. Significant disruptions are expected, and there are no alternative solutions. 3: Basic Access Route:An access route exists, but it is not fully flood-resistant. While it may remain operational under minor flood conditions, it is prone to disruption during more severe events, limiting its effectiveness in ensuring continued transportation. 4: Moderately Adequate Access Route:A well-planned access route is in place, likely to remain operational during most flood events. The route is generally reliable, though it may require additional support or maintenance to ensure continuous operation under extreme flood conditions 5: Full/Comprehensive and Flood-Resistant Access Route:A fully reliable and flood-resistant access route for public transportation is established. This route is highly likely to remain operational during and immediately after flood events, ensuring uninterrupted transportation for the public and emergency responders.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.6 Does the health care facility Have trees planted in a secure place that will not block access to the facility or fall on the building during an event?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.6 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No trees planted or unmanaged planting:Trees are planted without any consideration for safety, access, or structural risks. Trees are placed haphazardly, posing a high risk of blocking access or falling on the facility during adverse events. 2: Minimal consideration for tree planting locations:Some trees are planted, but there is limited planning around placement relative to the building or facility access. Moderate risk exists that trees could block access or pose a falling hazard during severe weather or other events. 3: Basic planning for tree planting, with some consideration for safety:Trees are planted with partial attention to avoiding interference with access or proximity to the building, but not all risks are mitigated. Occasional assessment of tree growth and placement occurs, but not enough to fully guarantee safety during events. 4: Moderate planning and foresight in tree planting:Trees are strategically placed to ensure they do not block access or pose a significant risk of falling on the facility. Regular maintenance and evaluation are conducted to monitor tree growth and structural integrity, reducing risk during events. 5: Full/Highly strategic and secure tree planting plan:Trees are planted in optimal locations, with comprehensive measures ensuring they will not block access or fall on the building during events. Ongoing monitoring, pruning, and management are fully integrated, ensuring long-term safety for both the facility and its access routes. Landscaping is done with a clear focus on safety, facility integrity, and event preparedness.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.7Does the health care facility Have estimates of the consumption (such as amount used per week) of essential medical, pharmaceutical, nutritional and laboratorial supplies, personal protective equipment,
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.7Does the health care fac
dataElements.description: 1: No estimates:No estimates are made for the consumption of essential supplies. There is no data or plan to address supply needs during a flood scenario. 2: Minimal estimates:Some awareness exists of the need to estimate consumption, but no formal data collection is conducted. There may be rough guesses, but they are not reliable or based on the likely flood scenario. 3: Basic estimates:Basic estimates of essential supply consumption are made, covering items like medical supplies, pharmaceuticals, and PPE. However, the estimates may not be detailed or based on robust analysis of the most likely flood scenario, leading to potential shortages or oversupply. 4: Moderate estimates:Well-structured estimates are in place, accounting for weekly consumption of medical, nutritional, and lab supplies, as well as PPE and food, using the most likely flood scenario. These estimates are reasonably accurate but may require further refinement to improve precision for extended flood situations. 5: Full/Comprehensive estimates:Detailed and comprehensive estimates are made for the weekly consumption of all essential supplies, including medical, pharmaceutical, nutritional, and PPE, based on the most likely flood scenario. The estimates are updated regularly, grounded in real data, and allow for efficient resource planning and stockpiling to ensure continuity of services during the flood.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.8 Does the health care facility Undertake risk assessments of the supply chain for essential medical and nonmedical products?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.8 Does the health care fa

1: No risk assessments of the supply chain are conducted:No processes or frameworks in place to evaluate vulnerabilities or disruptions in the supply chain for essential medical and nonmedical products. High vulnerability to supply chain disruptions, with no preparedness for mitigating risks. 2: Minimal or occasional risk assessments of the supply chain:Basic or informal evaluations may occur sporadically, but there is no systematic approach.
Some awareness of supply chain risks, but assessments are incomplete or narrowly focused on select areas, leaving significant gaps.
Limited mitigation measures for potential disruptions. 3: Basic Initial efforts to conduct risk assessments of the supply chain:Risk assessments cover key components of the supply chain for essential medical and nonmedical products, but may lack depth or integration. Some proactive identification of risks, with basic mitigation plans in place, though these are not comprehensive or fully operational. Limited follow-up or ongoing evaluation of risks. 4: Moderately Structured and systematic risk assessments are regularly conducted across the supply chain for both medical and nonmedical products:Comprehensive identification of potential risks, including vulnerabilities related to suppliers, logistics, and external factors. Regular reviews ensure the supply chain is resilient and adaptable to disruptions. 5: Fully/Highly advanced, proactive, and continuous risk assessments of the entire supply chain:Detailed and dynamic assessments cover all stages of the supply chain for essential medical and nonmedical products, with real-time monitoring of emerging risks. Mitigation strategies are robust, flexible, and regularly updated, incorporating contingency plans, alternative suppliers, and advanced forecasting techniques. The organization exhibits a high level of resilience to supply chain disruptions, with adaptive capabilities to handle risks proactively.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.9 Does the health care facility Have a secure plan to ensure continuity of the facility’s supply and delivery chain?
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.9 Does the health care fa
dataElements.description: 1: No plan exists for ensuring continuity of the facility’s supply and delivery chain:No processes or strategies in place to address potential disruptions. High vulnerability to supply chain interruptions, with no preparedness for ensuring continuity during emergencies or crises. 2: Minimal planning for supply and delivery chain continuity:Basic or informal strategies are in place, but they are incomplete, reactive, or poorly defined. There is limited awareness of risks, and plans are not comprehensive enough to cover all essential supplies. Continuity is mostly dependent on external conditions, with few or no contingency measures in place. 3: Basic, formalized planning for continuity of the supply and delivery chain:Some assessment of potential disruptions has been conducted, and basic contingency plans are in place for key supplies. Plans may still be underdeveloped for non-essential items, and there is limited testing or updating of the plan. Reliance on a few suppliers, with some effort made to develop alternative options. 4: Moderately Structured and comprehensive plans for ensuring continuity of the facility’s supply and delivery chain:The plan covers both essential and non-essential supplies, with clear processes for managing disruptions. Risk assessments and mitigation strategies are in place, including the identification of alternative suppliers, backup logistics options, and protocols for maintaining service levels. The plan is regularly reviewed and tested to ensure it is adaptable to various scenarios, and staff is trained on the plan’s execution. 5: Fully/Highly secure and dynamic plan for supply and delivery chain continuity:The plan is fully integrated into the facility’s operations and includes real-time monitoring of supply chain risks, with contingency strategies that cover a broad range of possible disruptions. The facility has multiple, vetted suppliers and alternative logistics arrangements, ensuring rapid adaptability during crises. Continuity plans are proactively updated based on emerging risks, industry trends, and lessons learned from past disruptions, making the supply chain highly resilient and responsive.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.10 Does the health care facility Have secure access to essential backup services, such as sterilization, laundry and cleaning services, via multiple agreements with different facilities to maintain funct
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.10 Does the health care f
dataElements.description:
1: No access to backup services for sterilization, laundry, or cleaning:No agreements in place with external facilities or partners to ensure continuity of critical services. The facility is highly vulnerable to disruptions, with no contingency plans for maintaining essential services during outages or failures. 2: Minimal or informal access to backup services:There may be one agreement or informal arrangements with a single external facility, but it lacks reliability and security. Backup services may be available on an ad hoc basis, but there is a significant risk of service interruption if the primary provider fails. No formal plans for handling multiple disruptions or ensuring continued access. 3: Basic access to backup services through limited agreements with external providers:There is some redundancy in place, with at least one formalized agreement for essential services (sterilization, laundry, cleaning), but the number of partners is small, and risks remain.
Contingency plans exist but may not cover all services or be fully comprehensive. Limited capacity to handle major disruptions affecting both the primary and backup providers. 4: Moderately Structured and secure access to essential backup services via multiple formal agreements with external facilities:Agreements cover all critical services (sterilization, laundry, and cleaning), with redundancy built in to ensure access during disruptions. Contingency plans are well-developed and regularly updated, allowing for seamless transitions between providers if one fails. The facility has confidence in maintaining critical services, though some risk may remain if multiple providers are affected simultaneously. 5: Fully/Highly secure and robust access to backup services, with multiple agreements in place across different external providers and facilities:Full redundancy is guaranteed, ensuring that sterilization, laundry, and cleaning services can continue uninterrupted, even during major disruptions.
Agreements are regularly tested, reviewed, and include real-time coordination to prevent service breakdowns.
The facility’s backup plans are proactively managed and fully integrated into its operations, providing maximum resilience against any potential service disruption.

dataElements.name: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.11 Does the health care facility Have secure access to essential backup food sources via multiple agreements with different vendors, and through cooperative agreements with other facilities to maintain f
dataElements.shortName: flood-infraTTP-shcfo-4.3.11 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No access to backup food sources:No agreements in place with vendors or other facilities to ensure food supply during disruptions. High vulnerability to food shortages or supply chain interruptions, with no contingency plans to maintain critical services during crises. 2: Minimal or informal access to backup food sources:There may be one vendor agreement, but it is not secure or guaranteed during a crisis. Backup food supply is ad hoc or reliant on a single source, with significant risks if the primary vendor cannot deliver. No formal cooperative agreements with other facilities or vendors for emergencies. 3: Basic access to backup food sources through limited agreements with vendors:At least one formal agreement exists with a backup vendor, but there may be gaps in coverage or redundancy. Contingency plans are in place, but they may not fully cover all food supply needs or critical services. Cooperative agreements with other facilities may be under development but are not yet fully operational. 4: Moderately Structured access to backup food sources through multiple agreements with different vendors:Formal contracts with multiple vendors provide a level of redundancy, ensuring food supply can be maintained during disruptions. Cooperative agreements with other facilities are in place, adding an extra layer of security for food sourcing. Contingency plans are well-developed and regularly reviewed to ensure continuity of critical services in case of food supply interruptions. 5: Fully/Highly secure access to backup food sources via multiple, well-established agreements with different vendors and facilities:Full redundancy is guaranteed, with multiple layers of contingency plans that ensure seamless food supply, even during significant disruptions. Proactive management of vendor agreements, with regular tests of the supply chain, cooperative arrangements, and real-time coordination among facilities to maintain uninterrupted food services. The facility is highly resilient, with a robust, fully integrated plan that mitigates all foreseeable risks to food supply and critical service functioning.
